title: Apparent Planckian scattering from local polaron formation
abstract: We propose a simple mechanism for apparent Planckian scattering based on local polaron formation, in which $Γ_\text{tr} = Γ_0 + αk_BT / \hbar$ with $α\sim O(1)$ emerges from quasielastic scattering without fine tuning. We provide evidence for our proposal in Monte Carlo simulations of the Holstein model with disordered electron-phonon coupling in the adiabatic limit. Our mechanism generates a finite interval of couplings in which the slope $α$ is approximately constant, coinciding with the onset of local polaron formation. In this regime, Matthiessen's rule is dramatically violated (or obeyed, depending on one's point of view) in that changes to the couplings, which in perturbation theory would alter the slope $α$, instead change the intercept $Γ_0$. We conjecture that a version of our mechanism applies to any system with a dominant disordered interaction that can drive polaron formation. This potentially includes regimes of the recently introduced disordered-Yukawa-coupling strange metal models where polaron formation is not suppressed.
summary: This paper proposes that the observed T-linear 'Planckian' scattering in certain metals can arise from the formation of local polarons in the presence of disordered electron-phonon coupling. Using Monte Carlo simulations of the Holstein model, the authors demonstrate that as coupling increases, the scattering rate slope saturates to a constant value. They show that this regime is characterized by a violation of Matthiessen's rule, where changes in coupling affect the intercept rather than the slope. The mechanism is local and does not depend on Anderson localization.
main_problem: Explaining the origin of 'apparent Planckian scattering' (T-linear scattering rates with a universal slope) without relying on complex many-body effects or fine-tuning.
main_result: The authors show that T-linear scattering emerges from local polaron formation in a disordered Holstein model, where the scattering rate slope saturates to a constant value as coupling increases.
method: Classical Monte Carlo simulations combined with Exact Diagonalization and a Local Approximation to treat the disordered electron-phonon coupling.
model_or_system: A 2D Holstein model in the adiabatic limit featuring spatially disordered electron-phonon coupling, where the coupling strength is drawn from a random distribution.
key_observables: Transport scattering rate (Gamma_tr), quasiparticle scattering rate (Gamma_qp), longitudinal optical conductivity, Drude weight, and Binder cumulant.
important_parameters: Adiabatic limit (omega_0 -> 0), dimensionless local coupling (lambda), temperature (T), and electron filling (nu).
main_assumptions: The study is restricted to the adiabatic limit; the local approximation assumes the joint distribution of phonon displacements is a product of local distributions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows T-linear scattering rates and the saturation of the slope alpha alongside Binder cumulants; Figure 2 displays distributions of effective site potentials across different coupling regimes; Figure 3 shows Monte Carlo convergence traces.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Planckian scattering problem, describes the disordered Holstein model, presents Monte Carlo/ED results demonstrating the emergence of T-linear scattering, analyzes the saturation of the slope alpha via polaron formation, and discusses the violation of Matthiessen's rule.
why_interesting: The mechanism provides a way to generate universal-like T-linear transport signatures from local, disordered interactions rather than requiring global many-body entanglement or fine-tuned critical points.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Scattering rates Γ = αT +Γ0 for the 2D Holstein model with static phonons and disordered γi ∼Uniform(−γmax, γmax), as a function of average dimensionless bare coupling λ = 1

FIG. 2. Distributions of effective site potentials qi = γixi (a–c) and Monte Carlo traces of the local electron density ⟨ni⟩(d–f) on representative sites with weak (a, d), intermediate (b, e), and strong (c, f) coupling λi at temperature T = 0.4t. Other model parameters are the same as in Fig. 1. Panels (a–c) show the distributions measured in Monte Carlo (MC) as well as fits to the MC using the local approximation (LA).

FIG. 3. Monte Carlo trace of xmax for four different phonon configuration initial conditions. Each trace consists of 50000 steps after an initial 5000 burn steps that are not shown. Quantitative diagnostics for each chain are shown in Table I.

FIG. 4. Scattering rates extracted by all methods and for all spatial directions (when relevant) for the two-dimensional square lattice with model parameters L = 20, t = 1, t′ = −0.3t, ν = 1.4. Quasiparticle and transport scattering rates are extracted from the quenched and annealed averaged spectral function and optical conductivity, respectively. All methods produce approximately T-linear scattering rates. The x- and y-direction extractions of the Drude and Drude weight (DW) methods agree with each other as expected since quenched disorder averaging restores the symmetry of the lattice.

FIG. 5. Quenched and annealed averaged spectral function evaluated near the nodal (a–c) and antinodal (d–f) k-points at representative values of the coupling and temperature for the two-dimensional square lattice with model parameters L = 20, t = 1, t′ = −0.3t, ν = 1.4.

FIG. 6. Quenched and annealed averaged optical conductivity in the x-direction (a–c) and y-direction (d–f) at representative values of the coupling and temperature for the two-dimensional square lattice with model parameters L = 20, t = 1, t′ = −0.3t, ν = 1.4. The x- and y- direction optical conductivities agree with each other as is expected since quenched disorder averaging restores the symmetry of the lattice.

FIG. 7. Density of states (a–c) and localization length of single particle eigenstates (d–f) at representative values of the coupling and temperature as a function of energy measured with respect to the chemical potential for the two-dimensional square lattice with model parameters L = 20, t = 1, t′ = −0.3t, ν = 1.4. The density of states is smoothed with a Gaussian kernel with standard deviation σ = 0.25. There remains a finite density of extended states at and near the chemical potential, indicating that our results are not driven by Anderson localization.
